relationship_corrections.reason: The cardinality 'some' is not standard; replaced with '*' to indicate multiple interfaces.
The cardinality 'some' is not standard; replaced with '*' to indicate multiple resources.
Multiple patients can have the same urgency level, so cardinality should be many-to-one.
Composition is not appropriate; each patient has exactly one record, so use one-to-one directed association.
Direction reversed: queue contains many patients, so queue should reference patient with one-to-many cardinality.
Composition arrow should have diamond on the whole side (queue), indicating one queue contains many patients.
Composition arrow should have diamond on the whole side (queue), indicating one queue contains many patients.
A resource can be used by multiple actors, so cardinality should be one-to-many.
A triage nurse can create multiple patient records, so cardinality should be one-to-many.

architecture_corrections.description: There are two queue classes: 'Queue' and 'PatientQueue'. The tasks consistently refer to 'PatientQueue' as the class owning the queue logic. The 'Queue' class is redundant and likely a leftover from an earlier design.

TriageNurse and Physician are both types of medical staff, but there is no inheritance relationship defined. According to the domain, they should be subclasses of MedicalStaff to reflect shared behavior and properties.
Similarly, Physician should be a subclass of MedicalStaff.

PatientQueue has three conflicting associations with Patient (a directed association with multiplicity, another directed association without multiplicity, and a composition). This is inconsistent and should be unified to a single composition indicating that PatientQueue owns Patients.
Remove the second conflicting directed association between PatientQueue and Patient.
Keep only the composition arrow from PatientQueue to Patient, which correctly models ownership.
Patient has two separate associations to UrgencyLevel (one with multiplicity and one without). The redundant one should be removed.

architecture_suggestions.suggestion: Replace SymptomResource with a value object called 'Symptom' that contains a name and optional severity. SymptomResource is misleading (sounds like a resource allocation) and should be a simple value object.
Rename 'State' to 'QueueState' or 'PatientState' to clarify its purpose. Currently it is ambiguous and could refer to queue status or patient status.

Consider making Patient the aggregate root that owns PatientRecord and UrgencyLevel as value objects. This would reduce external references and enforce consistency when registering and updating urgency.

The dashboard-related classes appear overly complex for the given requirements. Consider simplifying to a single 'Dashboard' class that aggregates queue data and calculates wait times, rather than modeling access operations as separate entities.
